startScreenOptions.title: PY8.1-PY8.7 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for PY8.1-PY8.7.

interactions.label: Which of the following INHIBITS the release of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) from t

params.question: Which of the following INHIBITS the release of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) from the posterior pituitary?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A rise in plasma osmolality is actually the strongest stimulus for ADH release. Osmoreceptors near the supraoptic nucleus detect increased osmolality and trigger secretion.
Hypovolaemia stimulates ADH release via baroreceptors in the atria and carotid sinus, helping the body retain water to restore volume.
Correct. Alcohol inhibits ADH secretion, which reduces water reabsorption in the collecting duct, leading to dilute urine and dehydration — the classic mechanism behind alcohol-induced diuresis.
Stress and pain are both recognised stimuli for ADH release, not inhibitors. This is one reason surgical patients may retain water postoperatively.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the stimuli and inhibitors of ADH. The key inhibitor discussed in this section is alcohol. All the other options — raised osmolality, low blood volume, and stress — are stimuli for ADH release.
Well done. Alcohol inhibits ADH release, which is why it acts as a diuretic and causes dehydration despite fluid intake.

interactions.label: T4 is the predominant secretory product of the thyroid gland, but T3 is three to

params.question: T4 is the predominant secretory product of the thyroid gland, but T3 is three to four times more biologically potent.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This statement is actually true. The thyroid secretes mostly T4 (~90%), but T3 is three to four times more potent. T4 is converted to T3 in peripheral tissues by deiodinase enzymes — this is why propylthiouracil, which blocks peripheral conversion, is useful in thyrotoxic crisis.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. T4 accounts for about 90% of thyroid output and acts as a prohormone. Peripheral deiodinases convert T4 to T3, which is the biologically active form with three to four times greater potency.

metadata.title: T4 is the predominant secretory product

interactions.label: Which zone of the adrenal cortex produces cortisol?

params.question: Which zone of the adrenal cortex produces cortisol?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The zona glomerulosa is the outermost zone and produces mineralocorticoids, primarily aldosterone, regulated by the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.

Correct. The zona fasciculata, the middle and largest zone, produces glucocorticoids — primarily cortisol. Remember the GFR mnemonic: Glomerulosa → mineralocorticoids, Fasciculata → glucocorticoids, Reticularis → androgens.
The zona reticularis is the innermost zone and produces adrenal androgens such as dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA), not cortisol.
The adrenal medulla is not part of the cortex. It is of neural crest origin and secretes catecholamines (adrenaline and noradrenaline), not steroid hormones.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember the GFR mnemonic for adrenal cortical zones (outside to inside): Glomerulosa makes mineralocorticoids (aldosterone), Fasciculata makes glucocorticoids (cortisol), and Reticularis makes androgens (DHEA).
Excellent. The zona fasciculata produces cortisol, which follows a diurnal rhythm peaking at 8 a.m. and has wide-ranging metabolic and anti-inflammatory effects.

interactions.label: Why does Type 2 diabetes mellitus typically NOT present with diabetic ketoacidos

params.question: Why does Type 2 diabetes mellitus typically NOT present with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)?

answers.text: Type 2 patients have lower blood glucose levels than Type 1 patients

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Type 2 patients can actually develop extreme hyperglycaemia (>600 mg/dL in HHS). The degree of hyperglycaemia does not determine whether ketoacidosis occurs.

answers.text: Residual insulin secretion in Type 2 is sufficient to suppress lipolysis and ketogenesis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. In Type 2 DM, beta cells still produce some insulin. This residual insulin is enough to suppress hormone-sensitive lipase in adipose tissue, preventing the massive lipolysis and ketone body formation that characterises DKA.

answers.text: Glucagon secretion is absent in Type 2 diabetes

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Glucagon secretion is not absent in Type 2 DM. In fact, inappropriate glucagon secretion contributes to the hyperglycaemia. The key difference is that residual insulin suppresses ketogenesis.

answers.text: Type 2 patients are resistant to the effects of ketone bodies

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: There is no resistance to ketone bodies in Type 2 DM. The reason DKA is uncommon is that residual insulin prevents the excessive fat breakdown that generates ketones in the first place.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key concept is residual insulin. In Type 1 DM, absolute insulin deficiency leaves lipolysis and ketogenesis unchecked, causing DKA. In Type 2 DM, even though insulin action is impaired, the remaining insulin is enough to suppress ketone production.

Well done. The critical distinction is that Type 2 DM retains enough endogenous insulin to suppress ketogenesis, even though it is insufficient to control blood glucose. This is why Type 2 presents with HHS rather than DKA.

interactions.label: Trousseau's sign is elicited by inflating a blood pressure cuff above systolic p

params.question: Trousseau's sign is elicited by inflating a blood pressure cuff above systolic pressure. What physiological mechanism underlies the resulting carpal spasm?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Although the cuff does occlude arterial flow, the spasm is not due to simple ischaemia. Healthy individuals do not develop carpal spasm with cuff inflation — it requires the pre-existing neuronal hyperexcitability of hypocalcaemia.

answers.text: Increased neuronal excitability due to low ionised calcium

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Low ionised calcium lowers the threshold for neuronal depolarisation, making peripheral nerves hyperexcitable. The transient ischaemia from cuff inflation further lowers ionised calcium locally, tipping already-excitable nerves into sustained firing and producing tetanic carpal spasm.

answers.text: Direct calcium depletion in skeletal muscle fibres

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Trousseau's sign reflects a neural phenomenon, not a primary muscle defect. The spasm results from increased peripheral nerve excitability causing involuntary motor discharge, not from calcium depletion within the muscle fibres themselves.

Sympathetic activation does not explain the specific pattern of carpal spasm. The mechanism is increased neuronal excitability from hypocalcaemia, which is unmasked by the ischaemic stress of cuff inflation.

overallFeedback.feedback: All clinical features of hypocalcaemia — tetany, Trousseau's sign, Chvostek's sign, perioral tingling, and seizures — trace back to one mechanism: increased neuronal excitability from reduced ionised calcium. The cuff-induced ischaemia unmasks this hyperexcitability.

Excellent. Low ionised calcium increases neuronal membrane excitability by lowering the depolarisation threshold. This is the unifying mechanism behind tetany, Trousseau's sign, Chvostek's sign, and the seizures seen in severe hypocalcaemia.

interactions.label: A patient presents with features suggestive of Cushing's syndrome. Which of the

params.question: A patient presents with features suggestive of Cushing's syndrome. Which of the following is the appropriate initial screening test?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The Short Synacthen test is a stimulation test used to diagnose adrenal insufficiency, not excess. Remember the principle: stimulation tests detect deficiency; suppression tests detect excess.

Correct. Dexamethasone is a synthetic glucocorticoid that should suppress ACTH and cortisol via negative feedback. In Cushing's syndrome, cortisol fails to suppress, confirming autonomous excess. This follows the principle that suppression tests detect hormone excess.

A single morning cortisol is useful for screening adrenal insufficiency (low value suggests deficiency), but it is unreliable for confirming Cushing's because cortisol naturally peaks in the morning and varies with stress.

ACTH helps localise the cause of Cushing's (pituitary vs ectopic vs adrenal) once the diagnosis is confirmed, but it is not the initial screening test. The overnight dexamethasone suppression test or 24-hour urine free cortisol is used first.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember the golden rule of endocrine testing: stimulation tests reveal deficiency, suppression tests reveal excess. For suspected Cushing's (cortisol excess), you need a suppression test — the overnight dexamethasone suppression test or 24-hour urine free cortisol.

Well done. The overnight dexamethasone suppression test applies the core endocrine testing principle: suppression tests detect excess. If exogenous glucocorticoid fails to suppress cortisol, autonomous overproduction is confirmed.
